coredata.dc:title: Constructing hollow ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor from Osmanthus leaves to specifically recognize bovine hemoglobin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiqing
Tian, Le
Ren, Runping
Wang, Tao
Wang, Yongqing
coredata.dc:description: To develop non-toxic, highly efficient and selective fluorescence sensors was a significance research. In this work, a novel hollow fluorescence sensor was designed with biomass carbon dots (CDs), ZIF-8 and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) via aqueous polymerization. The results demonstrated such unique structure fluorescence sensor exhibited fast response time, excellent stability and highly selectively towards bovine hemoglobin (BHb). Even in a complex environment, the hollow fluorescence sensor (H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs) still has a good recognition effect on BHb. Under an optimized condition, the hollow fluorescence sensor was quenched linearly with BHb concentration in the range of 0.058–4.5 μM with the detection limit of 15.6 nM. In addition, a possible quenching mechanism of hollow fluorescence towards BHb was confirmed resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the actual application process, the hollow fluorescence sensor showed a better detection performance towards BHb with the recoveries ranged of 98.6–101.1 %. This work provided a strategy to design green and unique hollow fluorescence sensor for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Hollow structure
Mechanism
Practical application

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of BHb by the sensor
Mechanism for detecting BHb by H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was maintained by Education Department of Jiangxi Province (GJJ211340, GJJ211339).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra of as-prepared samples (b).
SEM and TEM images of ZIF-8/CDs (a, b) and H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 (c, d).
XPS spectrum of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, (a) survey, (b) Zn 2p, (c) N 1s, (d) C 1s and (e) O 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum; (f) The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms.
The UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 (a), the fluorescence of H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2, H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 before and after elution (b).
The fluorescence response time to BHb (a) and stability (b) of different sensors.
(a) The effects of the additions amount of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and (b) the pH on the detection system.
Fluorescence spectra of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 (a) and H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 (b) after adding different concentrations of BHb, the responding linear relationship between fluorescence sensor and BHb (c).
The selectivity of H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 (a) and H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 (b) to other proteins.
Fluorescence responses towards BHb in different ions environments over H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2.
The UV–vis absorption spectra (a), fluorescence decay curves over H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 with BHb (b) and without BHb (c).
Comparisons of analytical performances with other reports for BHb detecting.
Detection results of BHb from spiked bovine urine sample (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Constructing hollow ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs fluorescent sensor from Osmanthus leaves to specifically recognize bovine hemoglobin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jingdezhen Ceramic University, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jingdezhen 333403, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jingdezhen Ceramic University, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jingdezhen 333403, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A hollow fluorescence sensor of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs was designed with biomass CDs and ZIF-8, which showed a better selectively and sensitivity towards BHb in real samples.

To develop non-toxic, highly efficient and selective fluorescence sensors was a significance research. In this work, a novel hollow fluorescence sensor was designed with biomass carbon dots (CDs), ZIF-8 and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) via aqueous polymerization. The results demonstrated such unique structure fluorescence sensor exhibited fast response time, excellent stability and highly selectively towards bovine hemoglobin (BHb). Even in a complex environment, the hollow fluorescence sensor (H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs) still has a good recognition effect on BHb. Under an optimized condition, the hollow fluorescence sensor was quenched linearly with BHb concentration in the range of 0.058–4.5 μM with the detection limit of 15.6 nM. In addition, a possible quenching mechanism of hollow fluorescence towards BHb was confirmed resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the actual application process, the hollow fluorescence sensor showed a better detection performance towards BHb with the recoveries ranged of 98.6–101.1 %. This work provided a strategy to design green and unique hollow fluorescence sensor for practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence detection, as a kind of indispensable analytical method, has displayed conspicuous benefit in quantifying trace levels of analytes owing to the technical simplicity, high sensitivity and low cost[1,2]. Currently, the fluorescent materials including fluorescent dyes, quantum dots (QDs) and rare earth metal have achieved excellent sensitive detection in recognizing targets. Among these, QDs have possessed higher stability and superior dispersed in water, which have received intensive attentions[3,4]. But some conventional QDs (e.g. CdTe, CdSe, InP, InAs etc.) existed the typical shortcomings like optical scintillation and cytotoxicity. To ameliorate the typical defects, carbon dots (CDs) as a kind of environmental-friendly zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanoparticles, had been explored by researchers at 2004[5]. After years of research, CDs have been found unique features such as low toxicity, high photostability, simple preparation methods, and excellent biocompatibility. These potentials make CDs superior to replace with conventional traditional QDs and fluorescent dye in fluorescent detection and bio-applications.

Up to date, CDs have been prepared by artificial or natural sources. But original natural products possessed merits of environmental protection, inexhaustible and low cost, gradually have been preferred to synthesize CDs. Since Liu et al. [6] firstly reported a green proposal for fabrication of CDs using grass as raw materials, researchers have used multifarious biomass materials (manure, flowers, wool, etc.) with different synthetic methods to prepare CDs[7–12]. For example, Wei et al. proposed a novel strategy to synthesize biomass CDs based on gynostemma as the carbon source via the calcination method[13]. Ensafi et al. reported a distinctive method through hydrothermal treatment using saffron as carbon precursor to prepare highly fluorescent CDs[14]. In their works, the natural raw materials as carbon sources prepared fluorescent CDs have exhibited tremendous potentials for bioimaging and sensing. However, there were small minority related reports have demonstrated the fluorescent CDs possessed the selective recognitions property without further modifications. To increase the practical applications of CDs, it would be extremely noteworthy to strategically ameliorate the selective recognition performance of CDs.

Molecularly imprinted technique (MIT) is an available and ideal option to improve fluorescence selectivity. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) were tailored polymeric composites via customized specific recognition sites which exactly matched in functional groups and shape with template molecules[15–18]. For example, a multifunctional molecularly imprinted sensor for single or simultaneous determination of hepatitis A virus and hepatitis B virus was provided by Luo et al[19]. He et al. reported n MIP-based assay for fluorescence detection of virus[20]. Thus, the fluorescence MIPs sensors could exhibit noticeable superiority to quantitative analysis of trace targets in complicated environments. Currently, lots of fluorescence sensors based on MIPs@QDs have been established for detection of various substances. Jia et al. have prepared a MIPs-based fluorescence sensor on the basis of CdTe QDs for the determination of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid[21]. Wang et al. have used green and red CdTe QDs combined with MIPs to construct a core–shell structured ratiometric MIPs-based sensor for determining bovine hemoglobin via naked-eyes[22]. Despite QDs materials and MIPs have been extensively utilized, a daunting challenge was existed correspondingly about the fluorescence of MIPs sensors have been quenched due to the quantum dots are extremely vulnerable agglomeration in the process of polymerization. This disadvantage would seriously hinder the practical applications of MIPs fluorescence sensors, which urgently needed to overcome.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), as a class of porous crystalline materials, have been intensively investigated in adsorption, catalysis, drug delivery and detection, owing to their large surface areas, porous structure, convenient functionalization and satisfactory stability[23–26]. Recently, significant explorations have been dedicated to the fabrication of zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) sensors for the various applications. Liu et al. have reported a In2O3/ZIF-8 nanofibers as sensor displayed extremely high response and more sensitivity than the pristine In2O3 nanofibers[27]. A fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor was designed by Yang et al. for Japanese encephalitis virus detection based on metal organic frameworks[28]. Bagheri et al. have prepared a molecular imprinted sensing materials based on bi-metal ZnCo MOF and magnetic graphene oxide, which have applied in ultrasensitive and selective detection for human serum atropine with a detection limit of 27 pg/mL[29]. These methods have commendably ameliorated the notorious aggregation-induced quenching effects of quantum dots.

Inspired by recent researches of MOF-supported sensors and molecular imprinting, a hollow ZIF-8/CDs fluorescence sensor with thin molecular imprinting (H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs) was prepared via aqueous polymerization. A series of characterization demonstrated that the novel H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs sensor has the rapid response time, higher selective and sensitive toward Bovine Hemoglobin (BHb) in complex environment due to the unique hollow structure and MIPs layer. Furthermore, the quenching mechanism was confirmed to be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) via fluorescence lifetime, UV–vis and Stern-Volmer equation. This work provided the importance information for understanding and exploring the MOF-supported sensors with molecularly imprinted technique.

The preparation of ZIF-8 was draw on the experience of other researchers with some modifications[30]. Primarily, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.05 mol) and Hmim (0.1 mol) were separately dissolved into 50 mL absolute methanol under ultrasound conditions. Next, the above solutions were thoroughly blended and stand for 1.0 h at 50 ℃ thermostat water bath. Subsequently, the white precipitate was centrifuged, washed with methanol and dried at 60 ℃ to obtain the ZIF-8 products.

The CDs were synthesized on the basis of our previously relevant reports by a simple hydrothermal route which the leaves were used as carbon sources and PEI served as nitrogen source[12]. The ZIF-8/CDs were synthesized via adsorption process. 1.0 mL CDs and 150 mg ZIF-8 were dispersed into 20 mL double distilled water (DDW) with ultra-sounding and vigorously stirring at room temperature. After 12 h, the resulting products were collected by centrifugation, washed and dried for the following experiments.

According to previously reported literature, the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs was constructed via a moderate route with modifications[31]. Firstly, 0.05 mmol hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) and 0.075 mmol resorcinol were dispersed into 10 mL DDW and agitated slowly for 40 min at 40 ℃. After that, 20 mg ZIF-8/CDs, 0.025 mmol HMTA and 0.075 mmol melamine were added. It should notice that the mixture was continuously stirred at low speed and instantly heated up to 80 ℃ for 30 min. When the reaction system was stopped heating and cooled to room temperature naturally, 0.125 μmol the template of BHb was put in the mixture and raised temperature to 40 ℃ for dissolving under 300 rpm. Later, the reaction system was kept at 40 ℃ and persisted for 24 h under static condition. Finally, the resultants were obtained through high-speed centrifugation and washed with the eluent (1.0 mol/L NaCl and 1.0 mol/L SDS solution were equal volume mixed) for 6.0 times. The sample was remarked as H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2. Meanwhile, the sample of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 and H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 was prepared by adjusting the raw material ratio (Table S1). In contrast, the H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 was fabricated through the same procedures without the addition of BHb template. The CDs@MIPs was also prepared with the above methods but the ZIF-8/CDs was replaced of CDs.

The fresh bovine urine sample was obtained from a robust bovine at local butcher (Zhenjiang, China), and then filtered with a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane aim at removing some suspended particulates. Moreover, the bovine urine sample was diluted 100-fold with PBS solution (pH = 7.4) for spiking and conducted the recovery experiments with known different amounts of BHb (0.15 mM, 0.375 mM, 0.6 mM and 2.4 mM) by H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, respectively.

As Fig. 1 a showed the XRD patterns of as-prepared ZIF-8, all the diffraction peaks matched well with the simulated patterns. The sharp and intensity of diffraction peaks indicated a purity and high crystallized ZIF-8 single crystals was prepared. The strong peaks at 7.3°, 10.4°, 12.7°, 14.7°, 16.5°, 18.1°, 24.4° and 26.7° were assigned to the (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (233) and (134) plans of ZIF-8, respectively. After CDs loaded, the diffraction peaks remain the similar peaks to ZIF-8 due to the quantum size of CDs, which was consistent with the previous reports[32]. Modifying with MIPs technique, the XRD patterns of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was still consistent well with the ZIF-8 without impurity, which attributed to the MIPs were tailored amorphous polymer composites. Moreover, compared to the XRD patterns of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 and H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 (Fig. S1), founding that the XRD patterns of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 was consistent well with the ZIF-8. For H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 (Fig. S1), the diffraction peaks of ZIF-8 was reduced, while the amorphous polymer was appeared, suggesting that the imprinted polymeric layer of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 was too thick. To demonstrate the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs materials, more characterizations were carried out.

FT-IR was used to further monitor the chemical composition of as-synthesized samples (Fig. 1b). For the FT-IR spectra of H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2, the peaks located at 690 and 755 cm−1 were ascribed to ZnN and ZnO in ZIF-8. The characteristics absorptions at 1000–1500 cm−1 related to the imidazole stretching vibrations of ZIF-8, confirming that ZIF-8 was prepared. Moreover, the bands at 2925, 1630 and 1144 cm−1 were assigned to the CH, CO and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CNHC of CDs respectively, which was consistent with the previous reports[33]. The other peaks appeared at 991, 1308 and 1455 cm−1 were assigned to NH, CN and CC stretching vibration, respectively, confirming the existence the polymerized polymers. Wonderful, the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 have the similar IR spectrum to H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 (Fig. 1b), demonstrating the BHb molecule was successfully eluted. The results again demonstrated that the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 have been designed.

The change of morphology could be observed by SEM and TEM images, which also could be used as evidence of the successful synthesis of materials. From the SEM and TEM images of ZIF-8/CDs (Fig. 2 a, b), it clearly observed that the ZIF-8/CDs was dodecahedron 3D structure with smooth surface, which was consistent with the bare ZIF-8 as previous reports[34]. The previous research has demonstrated that the addition of CDs does not affect the morphology of ZIF-8[32]. In addition, because of the size of CDs was too small and evenly distributed, the existence of CDs could not be found. For the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, the surface was becoming rough due to the imprinter layer was covered on the surface of ZIF-8/CDs. Meanwhile, the TEM image of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 further demonstrated that an imprinter layer was growth on the surface (Fig. 2c, d). Surprisingly, the middle of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was hollowed out to form a hollow structure. The hollow structure was attributed to the effect of phenolic acids, which could release free protons to destroy the framework of MOFs[35]. Such unique structure could enrich the target in the cavity to achieve sensitive detection. Thanks to the synergy of imprinter layer and hollow structure, the sensor has strong anti-interference and sensitivity.

At the same time, the morphology of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 and H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 was observed by SEM and TEM. In Fig. S2a, the SEM images of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 showed a ZIF-8 morphology with rough surface, which was attributed to the few imprinted layers. The TEM images of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 was further confirmed that there were fewer imprinter layers and no hollow structures were formed (Fig. S2c). When the dosage of raw materials was greatly increased, the morphology of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 was damaged (Fig. S2b, d). The damaged structure would not form a good support, which affected the dispersion of quantum dots, and was also not conducive to the imprinting layer the covering. Thus, this was important to prepare optimum H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 with appropriate dosage of reagents.

The elemental composition and chemical state of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 were analyzed by XPS. The survey XPS spectra (Fig. 3 a) showed the elements of Zn, C, N and O were presented in H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, suggesting the novel sensor material was successfully prepared. Moreover, the corresponding high-resolution spectra of elements Zn, C, N and O from H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 were shown in Fig. 3. For high-resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 1021.9 eV and 1045.1 eV could ascribe to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 respectively due to the Zn2+ in the ZIF-8[36]. As Fig. 3c showed, the peaks at 399.3 eV and 400.4 eV were assigned to pyridinic-N and quaternary-N of ZIF-8, respectively[37]. The XPS spectrum of Zn 2p and N 1s demonstrated the ZIF-8 was synthesized. The C 1s spectra could be divided with three peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.7 eV and 286.4 eV, which can be ascribed to CC, CO and CN, respectively (Fig. 3d). In the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3e), two peaks were found at 531.4 eV and 532.7 eV corresponding to OCO and CO/COC respectively[38,39]. These results further demonstrated that the sensor was obtained. In addition, the N2 adsorption–desorption analysis was applied to evaluate the specific surface area of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 sensor. As shown in Fig. 3f, the isotherm was a type-IV curve. The BET surface areas and pore diameter of the H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 was 951.351 m2/g and 1.259 nm, respectively (Fig. 3f and S3a). For H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, the BET surface area and pore diameter was determined to be 1432.133 m2/g and 2.593 nm, respectively (Fig. 3f and S3b). As expected, the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 showed higher surface areas due to the existence of empty cavities after the template was eluted. The results also demonstrated the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor was successfully designed with multiple recognition sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BHb by the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optical properties of the sensor, the UV–vis absorption and FL excitation/emission spectra of the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor were displayed in Fig. 4 a. The peak at about 360 nm was derived from n-π* transition of CO of CDs and n-σ* transition of groups like OH and NH2 [40,41]. These groups would promote the dispersion of sensor in aqueous solution and increased the contact probability of the target. Fig. 4a showed the optimal FL excitation and emission wavelengths of sensor at 357 nm and 439 nm, respectively. Thus, the optimal excitation wavelength of 357 nm was used for the whole experiments. As shown in Fig. 4b, comparing H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 sensor, no obvious change in fluorescence intensity. When BHb was present, the fluorescence intensity of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor decreased significantly. The results further confirmed that the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor has been eluted clean and has good fluorescence performance for BHb detection.

As well known that time determined efficiency, the response time of sensor toward BHb was the first defined. From the Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs instantly reduced with the addition of BHb and acquired the balance with in 10 min. Exciting was that the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor acquired a plateau within 5.0 min under the same condition, which was 2.0 times faster than CDs@MIPs. For H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 and H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3, the response rate was slower than H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 due to the structure. Although, ZIF-8/CDs acquired the balance with in 3.0 min, the lack of imprinted sites does not have the ability to selectively recognize. The fast response time of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor toward BHb was mainly attributed to the unique hollow structure, which was enriched the target in the cavity to achieve sensitive detection. The special structure can effectively disperse CDs and increase reaction contact sites toward BHb. Thus, H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor showed faster response time toward BHb. The stability of the as-prepared sensor toward BHb was studied by repeatedly measuring the fluorescence spectra. As the Fig. 5b showed, the fluorescence intensity values of F0/F remained the higher stability during 120 min. To further investigate the stability, the XRD pattern and fluorescence spectra were provided on H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 that has been placed for 6 months. As shown in Fig. S4, there was no significant change in XRD pattern and fluorescence intensity, suggesting that the sensor has good stability. Thus, the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 cloud used as a better sensor for recognition BHb.

Moreover, the sensor concentration and pH value can significantly influence on the sensitivity. With the increased amount of sensor, the fluorescence intensity was increased, while the sensitivity reached an extreme value. As Fig. 6 a showed, the sensitivity towards BHb achieved an extreme when the content of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was 36 mg/L. Thus, 36 mg/L was chosen as the concentration for the further testing. Between pH = 5–9, the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor exhibited good stability (Fig. 6b), indicating that the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor has good application potential. When BHb was added, the fluorescence intensity of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor has obvious changes under different pH value. As well known, protein structure was easy to denature under acidic and alkaline conditions, resulting in decrease in BHb concentration. It was worth nothing that H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor has the good stability and pH = 7.40 was the optimum conditions for the detection of biological macromolecules.

To further demonstrate the importance of structure to sensitivity of MIPs sensor, the fluorescence intensities was investigated by changed the amount of BHb under the optimal conditions. The linear relationship in fluorescence intensity ration of (F0/F) toward BHb concentration was presented in Fig. 7 and Fig. S5. The fluorescence intensity was decreased gradually with a gradual red-shift, which was attributed to the BHb absorption overlapped the excitation of sensor leaded to a higher quenching of the short-wavelength side. However, the sensor showed different degree of quenching, which was attributed to structural advantages. The fluorescence quenching data can be calculated based on the Stern-Volmer formula:
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
Where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of fluorescence probe in the absence and presence of the target, Ksv, kq and [Q] were the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, the quenching rate constant and target concentration.

Fig. 7a showed a good linear relationship of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 within 0.058–4.5 μM BHb and the corresponding linear curve equation was y = 1.404x + 0.918 (R2 = 0.9985). The limit of detection (LOD) of 15.6 nM (Table S2) was calculated according to the 3σ IUPAC criteria of 3σ/s[42]. Although, the CDs@MIPs (Fig. S5d, S6) and H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 (Fig. S5c, S6) were all showed good linear relationship, their LOD was 56.2 nM and 34.3 nM, respectively. The worse ability was caused by the lack of ZIF-8 as carrier and the structural damage. In addition, due to the lack of imprinted sites, the LOD of ZIF-8/CDs (Fig. S5a), H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 (Fig. S5b) and H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 (Fig. 7b) was 101.5 nM, 92.7 nM and 164.3 nM (Fig. S6, Table S2), respectively. In contrast, the imprinting factor[42] of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 was calculated, defined as linear slope ration, which was about 5.0 times higher than H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 (Fig. S6, Table S2), further suggesting the high imprinting effect of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2. Although CDs was the core of the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor, its detection sensitivity was still low in the absence of the synergy of MIPs layer and unique hollow structure. Thus, the unique hollow H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor has a better detection ability.

To verify recognition performance of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs sensor for detecting BHb, the number of biomacromolecule was examined, e.g. Cyt, Lys, Myosin and BSA. As shown in Fig. 8 , ZIF-8/CDs exhibited fluorescence to all substance due to no layers. On the contrary, no significant fluorescence response of H-ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 sensor to all substance due to the absence of imprinting effect (Fig. 8). For H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor (Fig. 8), it was noted that the fluorescence intensities were only changed toward to BHb, although they have the similar chemical behavior. For CDs@MIPs (Fig. 8), it could selectively identify BHb, but its ability to identify was limited without the support of ZIF-8. For all H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs (Fig. 8), the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 has the optimum detect performance due to proper regulation. Excluding the morphological different, the reason was ascribed to the selection recognitions sites existed in the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 imprinted layer and unique structure. Thus, H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 could be used as a fluorescence sensor for selective recognition of BHb.

More importantly, the H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor showed relatively low LOD and good linear ranges (Table S2), which was superior to some previous researches (shown in Table 1 ). In addition, no precious or toxic quantum dots were used in H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor. Therefore, the fluorescent sensor has the potential for the rapid analysis and recognition detection of BHb in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for detecting BHb by H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the fluorescence quenching process of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 by BHb, it was benefit to further modify and enhance the sensitivity of fluorescence probe. Hemoglobin (Hb), one of the Histidine (His)-rich proteins, is similar to bovine hemoglobin and consisted of two identical α and β chains to form a tetrameric protein (Fig. 10 )[50]. The helical structure of each polypeptide chain forms a hydrophobic space, and Fe2+ is located at the center of the heme porphyrin ring, forming a hexa ligand with the porphyrin ring on the polypeptide chain, O2 and the four pyrrolyl groups of histidine (Fig. 10).

As general, the BHb can be absorbed into the surface of sensor via π-π interactions. But Park’s group have demonstrated that the quenched fluorescence was attributed to the hydrophobic interactions not π-π interactions[51]. Meanwhile, the Fe2+ may also coordinate with sensor via the surface of hydroxyl groups. These factors could quench the fluorescence intensity of sensor due to the formation of strong affinity complex, resulting in higher binding constant and static quenching. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or Inner filter effect (IFE) could explain the quenching mechanism. Both mechanisms were generated because of filtering off emission by a second absorber, which a difference was that the distance of higher binding constant value between BHb and sensor cannot be higher 10 nm. Thus, IEF was not possible in this BHb system. Moreover, the three characteristics of Forster non-radiative energy transfer theory was observed in Fig. 11 a and Fig. 4a: (1) the dipole interactions between Fe2+ and hydroxyl groups, (2) the emission spectra of sensor were superimposed by the absorption spectrum of BHb, (3) the distances were less than 10 nm. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of this system was FRET.

Widely accepted that the quenching mechanism can be confirmed by fluorescence decay curves. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of sensor and sensor-BHb system were recorded. All data were will fitted with 3-exponential formula and the result was presented in Fig. 11b and c. The average fluorescence lifetime of sensor and sensor-BHb system was 26.26 ns and 9.32 ns respectively, which calculated according to the formula of Σbiτi [52]. For static quenching, the excited electron instantly returned to the ground state without photo emission due to formation of a non-fluorescence complex absorbed the excited light. In contrast, FRET occurred due to the excited-state energy of the donor transmitted to the acceptor. The fluorescence lifetime confirmed that the quenching mechanism was FRET process.

The proposed fluorescence sensor was applied to validate the practicability in real sample. A recovery experiment was conducted in the urine sample which collected from a healthy bovine and spiked with various amount of BHb. The results of H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 detecting BHb have obtained with satisfying recoveries in the range of 98.6–101.1 % and displayed in Table 2 . Moreover, the relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 5)were ranged from 2.38 % to 4.35 %. Therefore, it has well demonstrated that the fluorescence sensor was presented great accuracyand promising for the determination of BHb in real complex samples.

The ZIF-8/CDs nanocomposite was coated by MIPs layer and used as fluorescence sensor for detection of BHb. Comparing with CDs@MIPs sensor, the hollow H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs sensor showed a fast response time toward BHb due to the special porous structure of ZIF-8. The ZIF-8 also played the support to CDs and MIPs layer. The MIPs layer not only provided specific sites for BHb but also acted as the protector for sensor, thus H-ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs sensor showed highly stable and selectively to BHb in complex environment. The sensor showed a good linear for detection of BHb in the range of 0.058–4.5 μM with a LOD of 15.6 nM. In addition, the quenching mechanism was determined by experimental evidence as FRET because of the strong adsorption between sensor and BHb. This work provided a new analytical system to design MOFs and MIPs-based fluorescence sensor for stable, fast and selective detecting target.

Xiqing Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Le Tian: Writing – review & editing. Runping Ren: Writing – review & editing. Tao Wang: Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yongqing Wang: Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was maintained by EducationDepartmentofJiangxiProvince(GJJ211340, GJJ211339).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122121.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
